description: Breaks command/function names into concatenated string fragments using the + operator.  The PowerShell runtime reassembles the fragments at execution time, defeating simple string-matching detection rules.  Attackers use this to evade signature-based detections that look for known cmdlet names like Invoke-Expression.
native_purpose: PowerShell code obfuscation via token-level string concatenation that splits recognizable command names into unrecognizable fragments
known_behaviors: Command names split into concatenated string literals
Produces strings with multiple + operators joining fragments
Each fragment is individually meaningless but assembles to a valid command
Often combined with Invoke-Expression (IEX) to execute the result
indicators: Multiple string literals joined with + operators forming command names
Pattern: ('fragment1'+'fragment2'+'fragment3') resolving to known cmdlets
Call operator & or . preceding parenthesized concatenation
String fragments that individually do not match known commands

description: Inserts PowerShell escape characters (backticks `) into command and parameter names at positions where the tick is a no-op.  PowerShell treats backtick-letter sequences as escape characters, but most letters after a backtick resolve to themselves (e.g., `I = I).  This breaks signature matching without changing execution semantics.
native_purpose: PowerShell code obfuscation via backtick insertion into tokens, breaking string signatures while preserving execution behavior
known_behaviors: Backtick characters (`) scattered throughout command names
Commands remain syntactically valid and execute normally
Simple grep/regex for cmdlet names fails to match
Often combined with random casing for additional evasion
indicators: Backtick character (`) appearing inside command/parameter names
Regex: [a-zA-Z]`[a-zA-Z] within token boundaries
High density of backticks in a single command line
Backticks not preceding standard escape sequences (n, r, t, 0, a, b, f, v)
Known cmdlet names with backticks inserted at arbitrary positions

description: Stores command names or code fragments in variables, then invokes them via the call operator (& or .).  This indirection hides the actual command being executed from static analysis, since the variable assignment and invocation can be separated across lines.
native_purpose: PowerShell code obfuscation via variable indirection that hides command names behind variable references
known_behaviors: Command name stored in a variable ($var = 'Invoke-Expression')
Variable invoked with & (call operator) or . (dot-source operator)
Actual command name not visible at the invocation point
Variable name may be randomized or obfuscated
indicators: Variables assigned string values that resolve to known cmdlet names
Call operator & or . followed by a variable reference ($var)
Set-Variable / Get-Variable used to store and retrieve command names
Pattern: $<var> = '<cmdlet_name>'; & $<var>
Character array or [char] casting assembled into command names

description: Uses .NET string join, array indexing, or wildcard-based member access to construct command names dynamically.  Leverages [string]::join or -join operator with character arrays to build strings that resolve to valid PowerShell commands at runtime.
native_purpose: PowerShell code obfuscation via dynamic string assembly using .NET methods, array operations, or wildcard member resolution
known_behaviors: Use of [string]::join or -join to assemble command names from arrays
Character arrays ('I','E','X') joined into command strings
Wildcard patterns used in Get-Command to resolve cmdlets
Regex-based member access on .NET types
indicators: [string]::join used to assemble strings from character arrays
-join operator applied to character arrays forming command names
Get-Command with wildcard patterns (*ke-*pres*)
Character arrays with numeric values corresponding to ASCII cmdlet names
Regex: \[string\]::join\s*\(\s*['"].*?['"]\s*,

description: Constructs command names by casting integers to [char] type and concatenating the results.  Each character of the command name is represented as its ASCII/Unicode code point, making the command unrecognizable to pattern-matching detection.
native_purpose: PowerShell code obfuscation via [char] type casting that represents command characters as numeric code points
known_behaviors: Extensive use of [char]<int> expressions
Multiple [char] casts concatenated with + operator
Numeric values correspond to ASCII codes of command characters
Result string executed via IEX or call operator
indicators: Multiple [char]<integer> expressions in sequence
Regex: \[char\]\d{2,3} appearing multiple times in a command
Hex notation [char]0x## used to represent characters
Arrays of integers cast to [char[]] and joined
-join operator following character cast sequences

description: Randomizes the casing of command names, parameter names, and other tokens.  PowerShell is case-insensitive, so 'InVoKe-ExPrEsSiOn' is functionally identical to 'Invoke-Expression'.  This defeats case-sensitive string matching rules.
native_purpose: PowerShell code obfuscation via random casing of tokens that defeats case-sensitive signature matching
known_behaviors: Mixed upper/lower case throughout command names
Parameter names with randomized casing
PowerShell keywords (.NET types, operators) with random casing
Functionally identical to original but visually different
indicators: Irregular casing pattern within single tokens (mixed case)
More than 2 case transitions within a single command name
Regex: [a-z][A-Z][a-z][A-Z] or similar alternating case patterns
Statistical anomaly: random distribution of upper/lower case
Known cmdlet names with non-standard casing

description: Applies string concatenation at the entire-command level, breaking the full script/command into multiple concatenated string pieces that are reassembled and executed.  Unlike token-level concatenation, this operates on the entire command string including arguments.
native_purpose: PowerShell code obfuscation via whole-command string concatenation that fragments the entire script into joined pieces
known_behaviors: Entire command broken into concatenated string literals
Multiple + operators joining strings that form a complete command
Result passed to IEX/Invoke-Expression for execution
May include whitespace fragments and escape characters
indicators: Long sequences of string concatenation with + spanning entire commands
Result of concatenation piped to IEX or passed to Invoke-Expression
String fragments that individually contain partial command syntax
Regex: ('[^']{1,10}'\s*\+\s*){3,} (many short fragments joined)
No single string literal contains a complete recognizable command

description: Uses PowerShell's -f (format) operator to reorder string fragments.  The command is split into pieces placed out of order, then the format string specifies the correct reassembly order using {0},{1},{2} placeholders.  This makes static analysis extremely difficult because the string fragments appear in a scrambled sequence.
native_purpose: PowerShell code obfuscation via format-operator string reordering that scrambles command fragments and reassembles at runtime
known_behaviors: Use of -f format operator with {N} placeholders
Command fragments appear out of order in source
Format string defines the correct reassembly sequence
Result executed via IEX or call operator
indicators: Format operator -f with {N} placeholders in strings
Regex: '\{\d+\}[^']*' followed by -f and comma-separated string arguments
Multiple string arguments after -f operator
Placeholder indices that suggest reordering (non-sequential like {2}{0}{1})
Result of format operation fed to IEX/Invoke-Expression

description: Reverses the entire command string, then uses array reversal and -join to reconstruct the original command at runtime before passing it to IEX.  The reversed string is completely unreadable and does not match any signature patterns.
native_purpose: PowerShell code obfuscation via string reversal that stores the command backwards and reverses it at execution time
known_behaviors: Command string stored in reverse character order
Array reversal ([array]::Reverse or indexing [-1..-N]) used to restore
-join operator reassembles characters into executable string
Result passed to IEX for execution
indicators: Reversed strings that when reversed form valid PowerShell commands
Array indexing with negative ranges [-1..-N] or [-1..-(Length)]
[array]::Reverse() calls on character arrays
-join applied after array reversal
Regex: \[-1\.\.\-\(? to detect reverse indexing patterns

description: Encodes the entire command as a Base64 string and decodes it at runtime using [Convert]::FromBase64String or PowerShell's native -EncodedCommand parameter.  Base64 encoding is the most common PowerShell obfuscation method and is frequently the outermost layer in multi-layer obfuscation.
native_purpose: PowerShell code obfuscation via Base64 encoding that converts the command to an encoded string decoded at execution time
known_behaviors: Base64-encoded strings decoded at runtime
Use of [Convert]::FromBase64String() or [System.Convert]::FromBase64String()
Use of -EncodedCommand (-enc) parameter with powershell.exe
Decoded bytes converted to string via [System.Text.Encoding]::Unicode
Result passed to IEX for execution
indicators: Base64-encoded strings (A-Z, a-z, 0-9, +, /, = padding)
-EncodedCommand or -enc parameter on powershell.exe
[Convert]::FromBase64String() calls
[System.Text.Encoding]::Unicode.GetString() decoding Base64 output
Regex: [A-Za-z0-9+/]{20,}={0,2} (Base64 pattern with min length)
Long single-string arguments that appear to be encoded data

description: Converts each character of the command to its hexadecimal representation (0x##) and reconstructs the string at runtime by converting hex values back to characters.  This produces long sequences of hex literals that are opaque to casual inspection.
native_purpose: PowerShell code obfuscation via hexadecimal encoding that represents each command character as a hex value
known_behaviors: Hex values (0x##) used to represent each character
Array of hex values converted to [char] type
-join operator assembles characters back into string
Result executed via IEX
indicators: Sequences of hex values: 0x##, 0x##, 0x## in arrays
Hex string split into pairs and converted via [char][int]"0x$_"
ForEach-Object converting hex integers to [char]
Long hex-encoded strings being decoded character by character

description: Encodes each character as its octal representation and reconstructs the command at runtime.  Less common than hex encoding but equally effective at hiding command content from signature-based detection.
native_purpose: PowerShell code obfuscation via octal encoding that represents command characters as octal values
known_behaviors: Octal values used to represent characters
Conversion from octal to decimal to char at runtime
Array of octal strings processed through conversion pipeline
Result assembled and executed via IEX
indicators: [convert]::ToInt32($_, 8) indicating base-8 (octal) conversion
Sequences of 3-digit numeric strings in the 100-177 range (printable ASCII octal)
Regex: \[convert\]::ToInt32\(.+?,\s*8\)
Arrays of quoted numbers converted to characters via octal
ForEach-Object pipeline converting octal strings to chars

description: Converts each character to its binary (base-2) representation and reconstructs at runtime.  Binary encoding produces very long strings of 0s and 1s that are completely opaque.  The decoder converts each 8-bit group back to a character.
native_purpose: PowerShell code obfuscation via binary encoding that represents each character as an 8-bit binary string
known_behaviors: Long strings of 0s and 1s representing binary-encoded characters
Each 8-bit group maps to one ASCII character
Conversion uses [convert]::ToInt32($_, 2) for base-2 decoding
Result assembled into string and executed via IEX
indicators: [convert]::ToInt32($_, 2) indicating base-2 (binary) conversion
Sequences of 8-digit strings containing only 0 and 1
Regex: ['"]0[01]{7}['"] appearing in arrays
Regex: \[convert\]::ToInt32\(.+?,\s*2\)
Very long strings of binary digits split into 8-character groups

description: Abuses PowerShell's SecureString infrastructure to encode commands.  ConvertTo-SecureString and ConvertFrom-SecureString are designed for credential management, but attackers use them to encode arbitrary command strings, making the encoded output appear as encrypted credential data rather than obfuscated code.
native_purpose: PowerShell code obfuscation via SecureString APIs that disguise command strings as encrypted credential data
known_behaviors: ConvertTo-SecureString used to create SecureString from command text
ConvertFrom-SecureString produces hex-encoded encrypted output
Decryption reverses the process to recover original command
Uses DPAPI or explicit key for encryption/decryption
indicators: ConvertTo-SecureString and ConvertFrom-SecureString in non-credential context
[Runtime.InteropServices.Marshal]::SecureStringToBSTR() calls
[Runtime.InteropServices.Marshal]::PtrToStringAuto() calls
SecureString output (long hex strings) being converted back for execution
Regex: ConvertTo-SecureString.*ConvertFrom-SecureString in same pipeline

description: XOR-encodes each byte of the command with a key value, then decodes at runtime by XORing again with the same key.  XOR encoding is symmetric (encode == decode) and commonly used in malware for payload obfuscation.  The key can be a single byte or a repeating multi-byte key.
native_purpose: PowerShell code obfuscation via bitwise XOR encoding with a key that scrambles command bytes and reverses at runtime
known_behaviors: Byte array XORed with a key value
-bxor operator used for bitwise XOR operations
Numeric arrays that decode to command strings when XORed
Key value may be embedded in the script or derived dynamically
indicators: -bxor operator applied to byte arrays
Regex: -bxor\s+\$?\w+ or -bxor\s+0x[0-9a-f]+
Arrays of integers being XORed and converted to characters
Symmetric XOR pattern: data XORed with key produces readable output
ForEach-Object pipeline applying -bxor to each element

description: Converts each character to its decimal ASCII code and reconstructs at runtime.  Similar to hex encoding but uses decimal values.  Each character is represented as an integer (e.g., 87 = 'W', 114 = 'r') and converted back via [char] casting.
native_purpose: PowerShell code obfuscation via decimal ASCII encoding that represents characters as their decimal code point values
known_behaviors: Arrays of decimal integers representing ASCII character codes
Each integer cast to [char] to recover the original character
Values in the 32-126 range (printable ASCII)
Result assembled and executed via IEX
indicators: Arrays of integers in the 32-126 range cast to [char]
Pattern: [char[]](N,N,N,...) -join '' where N are 2-3 digit integers
ForEach-Object converting integers to [char]
Environment variable indexing ($env:ComSpec[4,15,25]) used to extract characters

description: Compresses the command using the Deflate algorithm, then wraps it in a PowerShell decompression cradle that inflates and executes at runtime.  The compressed data is typically Base64-encoded for transport.  This creates a multi-layer obfuscation (compress + encode) that requires two stages of processing to reveal the original command.
native_purpose: PowerShell code obfuscation via Deflate compression that compresses the command and wraps it in a decompression/execution cradle
known_behaviors: System.IO.Compression.DeflateStream used to decompress at runtime
Compressed data stored as Base64 string
[IO.StreamReader] reads decompressed stream
Decompressed output passed to IEX for execution
Characteristic nested New-Object calls for stream handling
indicators: IO.Compression.DeflateStream in command text
CompressionMode::Decompress enumeration value
IO.MemoryStream wrapping FromBase64String output
IO.StreamReader reading from decompression stream
Regex: DeflateStream.*FromBase64String.*Decompress
Nested New-Object calls creating compression pipeline

description: Compresses the command using Gzip algorithm and wraps it in a decompression cradle.  Similar to Deflate but uses GzipStream instead of DeflateStream.  Gzip adds a header and checksum over the Deflate-compressed data.
native_purpose: PowerShell code obfuscation via Gzip compression that compresses the command and wraps it in a GzipStream decompression cradle
known_behaviors: System.IO.Compression.GzipStream used for decompression
Compressed data stored as Base64-encoded string
Decompression pipeline identical to Deflate except for stream type
Result read via StreamReader and executed via IEX
indicators: IO.Compression.GzipStream in command text
Gzip magic bytes in decoded Base64 (starts with H4sI in Base64)
CompressionMode::Decompress with GzipStream
Regex: GzipStream.*FromBase64String.*Decompress
Same decompression pipeline pattern as Deflate but with GzipStream
Base64 strings starting with H4sI (Gzip header 0x1F8B)

description: Obfuscates the PowerShell.exe launcher itself using path variations, abbreviated parameter names, environment variable resolution, and alternative invocation methods.  PowerShell accepts heavily abbreviated parameters (-e for -EncodedCommand, -w h for -WindowStyle Hidden) and can be invoked via full path, partial path, or SilentlyContinue through environment variables.
native_purpose: PowerShell launcher obfuscation via executable path variations and parameter abbreviation to disguise powershell.exe invocation
known_behaviors: powershell.exe invoked via full path, partial path, or environment variables
Parameter names abbreviated to minimum unambiguous prefix
WindowStyle Hidden abbreviated to -w h or -wi hi
-NoProfile, -NonInteractive, -ExecutionPolicy Bypass abbreviated
Alternative powershell paths: $PSHOME\powershell.exe, SysWOW64 path
indicators: powershell.exe with heavily abbreviated parameters (-nop, -w h, -enc, -ep)
Full path to powershell.exe in System32 or SysWOW64
String insertion in 'powershell' (pow"er"shell, pow`er`shell)
$PSHome variable used to locate powershell.exe
-WindowStyle Hidden (or abbreviated forms -w h, -wi hi, -win hid)

description: Wraps the PowerShell execution inside cmd.exe using /c or /k flags.  This adds an extra process layer between the initial execution and the PowerShell process, complicating process-tree-based detection.  Cmd.exe can also apply its own obfuscation (caret insertion, environment variable substitution).
native_purpose: PowerShell launcher obfuscation via cmd.exe wrapping that adds a process layer and enables cmd-level obfuscation techniques
known_behaviors: cmd.exe /c or /k launching powershell.exe
Extra process in process tree (cmd -> powershell)
Cmd.exe caret (^) insertion in the powershell command
Cmd.exe environment variable substitution (%COMSPEC%)
Multiple nested cmd /c calls for additional layers
indicators: cmd.exe or cmd /c preceding powershell invocation
Caret characters (^) inserted into command tokens
%COMSPEC% environment variable used as cmd.exe alias
Nested cmd /c calls creating multiple process layers
Regex: cmd(\.exe)?\s+/[ck]\s+.*powershell
Process tree: cmd.exe -> powershell.exe

description: Uses wmic.exe process call create to spawn PowerShell.  WMIC is a legitimate system management tool, so its execution may not trigger the same alerts as direct PowerShell invocation.  The command string is passed as an argument to process creation.
native_purpose: PowerShell launcher obfuscation via WMIC process creation that spawns PowerShell through the WMI management interface
known_behaviors: wmic.exe used to create a new process running PowerShell
Process tree shows wmic.exe as parent of powershell.exe
WMI event traces in Windows event logs
The launched command appears as a WMI process creation
command_examples: wmic process call create "powershell -nop -w h -enc VwByAGkAdABlAA=="
wmic /node:localhost process call create "powershell -ep bypass -c IEX(whoami)"
indicators: wmic.exe process call create followed by powershell command
Regex: wmic.*process\s+call\s+create.*powershell
WMI process creation event logs (Event ID 4688 with wmic parent)
wmic /node parameter targeting localhost or remote hosts
Process tree: wmic.exe -> powershell.exe

description: Uses mshta.exe with inline VBScript to launch PowerShell.  Mshta is the Microsoft HTML Application Host, and it can execute VBScript or JScript inline.  The VBScript creates a WScript.Shell object and runs PowerShell through it, adding multiple process layers.
native_purpose: PowerShell launcher obfuscation via mshta.exe VBScript execution that spawns PowerShell through the HTML Application Host
known_behaviors: mshta.exe executing inline VBScript with vbscript: protocol
VBScript Execute() or CreateObject("WScript.Shell") to run PowerShell
Multiple process layers: mshta -> wscript -> powershell
May use javascript: protocol as alternative
indicators: mshta.exe with vbscript: or javascript: protocol handler
Regex: mshta\s+(vbscript|javascript):
CreateObject("WScript.Shell") in mshta command line
ActiveXObject('WScript.Shell') in JavaScript variant
Process tree: mshta.exe -> powershell.exe
close keyword at end of VBScript execution block

description: Uses rundll32.exe with the mshtml RunHTMLApplication entry point to execute JavaScript that launches PowerShell.  Rundll32 is a trusted Windows binary, so it may bypass application whitelisting.  The JavaScript variant uses ActiveXObject to create a shell and run PowerShell commands.
native_purpose: PowerShell launcher obfuscation via rundll32.exe JavaScript execution that spawns PowerShell through the RunHTMLApplication entry point
known_behaviors: rundll32.exe loading mshtml.dll RunHTMLApplication
JavaScript executed inline via rundll32
ActiveXObject('WScript.Shell') used to spawn PowerShell
Process tree: rundll32.exe -> powershell.exe
indicators: rundll32.exe with javascript: protocol and mshtml,RunHTMLApplication
Regex: rundll32.*javascript:.*mshtml.*RunHTMLApplication
document.write() in rundll32 command line
ActiveXObject("WScript.Shell") in rundll32 context
URL-encoded spaces (%20) in JavaScript payload
Process tree: rundll32.exe -> powershell.exe

description: Uses cscript.exe or wscript.exe to execute a VBScript or JScript file (or inline script) that launches PowerShell.  These are the Windows Script Host engines and are commonly used in legitimate administration.  The script creates a Shell object and executes the PowerShell command through it.
native_purpose: PowerShell launcher obfuscation via Windows Script Host (cscript/wscript) that executes a script spawning PowerShell
known_behaviors: cscript.exe or wscript.exe executing .vbs/.js file
Script file creates WScript.Shell and runs PowerShell
Process tree: cscript/wscript -> powershell.exe
May write temporary .vbs/.js file to disk or use //e:vbscript engine
indicators: cscript.exe or wscript.exe executing scripts that spawn PowerShell
Temporary .vbs or .js files written before execution
//e:vbscript or //e:jscript engine selection flags
CreateObject("WScript.Shell").Run with PowerShell in arguments
Process tree: cscript.exe/wscript.exe -> powershell.exe
Regex: (cscript|wscript).*powershell
